records.ety: [F. saillir, fr. L. salire to leap, spring, akin to Gr. "a`llesqai; cf. Skr. s to go, to flow. Cf. Salient, Assail, Assault, Exult, Insult, Saltation, Saltire.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Sallied (-ld); p. pr. & vb. n. Sallying.]

defs: To leap or rush out; to burst forth; to issue suddenly; as a body of troops from a fortified place to attack besiegers; to make a sally.
quotes: They break the truce, and sally out by night.
The foe retires, -- she heads the sallying host.

records.ety: [F. saillie, fr. saillir. See Sally, v.]

defs: A leaping forth; a darting; a spring.
A rushing or bursting forth; a quick issue; a sudden eruption; specifically, an issuing of troops from a place besieged to attack the besiegers; a sortie.
quotes: Sallies were made by the Spaniards, but they were beaten in with loss.

defs: An excursion from the usual track; range; digression; deviation.
quotes: Every one shall know a country better that makes often sallies into it, and traverses it up and down, than he that . . . goes still round in the same track.

defs: A flight of fancy, liveliness, wit, or the like; a flashing forth of a quick and active mind.
quotes: The unaffected mirth with which she enjoyed his sallies.

defs: Transgression of the limits of soberness or steadiness; act of levity; wild gayety; frolic; escapade.
quotes: The excursion was esteemed but a sally of youth.
